title: The Didarganj Yakshi's Polished Secret
subtitle: A riverbank revelation and the mystery of a mirror-bright queen of stone
era: Early historic sculpture, debated Mauryan or Kushan date
region: Didarganj near Patna; Bihar Museum, Patna
moral: Honest wonder leaves room for unanswered questions.

sources.citation: Didarganj Yakshi, found at Didarganj Kadam Rasual near Patna on October 18, 1917
sources.note: Physical Chunar sandstone sculpture with famous mirror-like polish, now in Bihar Museum.

sources.citation: James C. Harle, The Art and Architecture of the Indian Subcontinent (1994)
sources.note: Art-historical basis for questioning the traditional Mauryan date.

sources.citation: Bihar Museum, Patna, updated early historic display interpretation
sources.note: Current cautious museum framing after the sculpture's move from Patna Museum.

panels.shot: epic low-angle wide of towering sculpture against darkened museum hall

dialogue.text: In the age of empires and rivers, the Didarganj Yakshi rose from mud and memory, her shining stone defying time and refusing a simple date.

panels.shot: serene wide shot of the Ganga near Patna at dawn

dialogue.text: On an October morning in 1917, the Ganga flowed past Didarganj as it had for ages, carrying silt, stories, and secrets buried on her banks.

panels.shot: medium shot of village men working on the riverbank

dialogue.text: Villagers cutting into the bank found stone where they expected only earth, their spades ringing against a hidden form.

panels.shot: dramatic low-angle as the upper torso of the Yakshi emerges from mud

dialogue.text: As hands scraped away mud, a woman of stone appeared—tall, serene, and shining even through the clay that veiled her.

panels.shot: slow push-in on the Yakshi’s polished surface catching light amid mud

dialogue.text: Beneath the river’s stain lay Chunar sandstone worked to a gleam so deep it threw back light like still water.

panels.shot: frontal medium shot showing full figure with fly-whisk

dialogue.text: Life-sized, bearing a fly-whisk, she seemed a celestial attendant suddenly returned to human sight from some older court.

panels.shot: museum-style side profile showing ornaments and forelock

dialogue.text: Her forelock, her ornaments, the faint sweep of cloth across her body—every detail carried the touch of a sculptor thinking in style, not only in shine.

panels.shot: wide interior shot of early Patna Museum hall

dialogue.text: Carried from the riverbank to Patna Museum, she entered the new world of labels and catalogues, an ancient figure now under modern eyes.

panels.shot: scholarly medium shot with curator pointing at Yakshi

dialogue.text: Her mirror polish, famed on the pillars of Ashoka, led many to mark her as Mauryan glory—an imperial lady of the third century BCE.

panels.shot: slow push-in on Yakshi’s head as pages of a scholarly book dissolve around

dialogue.text: Yet art historians like James C. Harle looked again, weighing not only her shine, but her hair, jewelry, and drapery against other early historic yakshis.

panels.shot: analytical montage: comparative silhouettes of other yakshi sculptures

dialogue.text: Her heavy forelock, certain ornaments, and the way cloth clings and falls seemed closer to yakshi figures from the Kushan centuries, long after the Mauryas fell.

panels.shot: medium shot of Yakshi under changing date labels

dialogue.text: Scholars argued, dates moved. Chunar sandstone could be polished for generations. Shine alone could not pin her to Ashoka’s court.

panels.shot: close-up of Yakshi’s polished torso beside a polished pillar fragment

dialogue.text: The famed Mauryan polish lived on in later workshops. The Yakshi’s gleam spoke of skill and patronage, but not of a single royal date.

panels.shot: somber wide shot of Yakshi in foreign gallery with nose subtly chipped

dialogue.text: On distant travels in the 1980s, her nose was damaged, a reminder that fragile stone carries not just beauty, but the risk of modern harm.

panels.shot: reverent wide shot of Bihar Museum gallery

dialogue.text: Returned and rehoused at Bihar Museum, she stands within an early historic gallery, surrounded by objects that share her long, uncertain span of time.

panels.shot: close shot of a modern museum label near Yakshi

dialogue.text: Now the caption steps back from certainty, marking her as early historic, her date debated between Mauryan and Kushan worlds.

panels.shot: medium shot of visitors observing Yakshi thoughtfully

dialogue.text: Visitors lean close, reading the questions, then look up at her, letting wonder sit beside doubt rather than forcing an easy answer.

panels.shot: intimate close-up of Yakshi’s calm face

dialogue.text: You may not know when I was born, only that I endured.

panels.shot: final wide shot of Yakshi silhouetted against timeline mural

dialogue.text: The Didarganj Yakshi stands between dates, teaching that honest history holds questions close, and that true wonder needs no simple, certain label.
